evidence_quotes.quote: Lootboxes and similar monetization mechanics introduce gambling-like experiences within games, often marketed to or accessible by minors.
the customer "purchased a license" that can be revoked at the company's leisure.

paragraphs.text: I would like to bring attention to additional issues that the Digital Fairness Act may wish to address: - Preservation of software functionality in digital products ("Stop Killing Games" movement): The Act should consider requiring that software products, including video games, remain usable even after certain online services not strictly required for its core functionality are discontinued. Increasingly, software depends on online-only Digital Rights Management (DRM) or external servers for certain functionality, meaning consumers lose access to products they purchased once these services shut down. Usually, this is done by forcing the customer to accept an End-User license agreement (EULA) that states the customer "purchased a license" that can be revoked at the company's leisure. Measures to ensure a minimal offline functionality, or the removal of non-essential DRM upon end-of-life would align with consumer rights and product durability. The catalyst for the "Stop Killing Games" movement was the Ubisoft title "The Crew", which despite being playable in single player is no longer playable because the servers were discontinued. - Gambling mechanics in games accessible to minors (e.g., "lootboxes" & "Gacha games"): Lootboxes and similar monetization mechanics introduce gambling-like experiences within games, often marketed to or accessible by minors. While some Member States classify these as gambling and regulate them accordingly, enforcement is inconsistent and limited in reach. A harmonized EU-wide approach would protect vulnerable consumers and reduce regulatory fragmentation. Moreover, adult consumers would also benefit from increased transparency and fairness in digital marketplaces. - Content-based restrictions by digital payment processors: While possibly tangentially related, I wanted to bring it up since it concerns payment, a core aspect of digital services. Certain dominant payment processors are imposing restrictions on platforms based on the nature of their legal content, effectively limiting access to financial infrastructure. While it's understandable that illegal content must be restricted, further scrutiny is needed when legal businesses are denied service based on subjective content guidelines. These processors serve as essential infrastructure in the digital economy and should not unilaterally determine what legal products or services consumers may access. The EU clearly considers similar issues in its Digital Markets Act (DMA). Payment processors are however not considered a core service. Given the considerable amount of leverage these companies have on digital businesses - who often have no viable alternative to collect payment - it would seem prudent that these payment providers are not allowed to police content they do not agree with but is otherwise legal. Lobbying organisations are actively abusing this by having payment providers strong-arm other companies into adjusting their content policies.
